content.text: Every element in a web page is rendered as a rectangular box. The CSS box model describes the four nested areas that make up that box: content, padding, border, and margin. Understanding how these interact — and how box-sizing changes the width calculation — is foundational to writing predictable layouts.

attrs.alt: CSS Box Model — content, padding, border, margin layers with box-sizing comparison

content.text: The Four Areas
Content

The content area holds the element's actual content — text, images, or child elements. Its dimensions are set by the width and height properties. The background-color and background-image of an element fill the content area and the padding area by default.
Padding
Padding is the transparent space between the content and the border. It inherits the element's background color. Padding can be set on all four sides individually or with shorthand:
Border
The border wraps the padding. It is drawn between the padding and the margin. Border is set with shorthand width, style, color — the style value is required, or the border is invisible:
border-style value
Appearance
solid
A single continuous line (most common)
dashed
A series of dashes
dotted
A series of dots
double
Two parallel lines
none
No border (default)
Margin
Margin is the transparent space outside the border. It separates the element from its neighbors. Like padding, margin accepts the same 4-value shorthand:
margin: auto on a block element horizontally centers it within its parent — but only works when the element has an explicit width.
box-sizing — The Width Calculation
The box-sizing property controls how width and height are measured.
content-box (the default)
With content-box, width and height apply to the content area only. Padding and border are added on top of the declared width, making the element larger than expected:
border-box (modern standard)
With border-box, width and height include content, padding, and border. The element renders at exactly the declared width — padding and border shrink the content area instead:
The universal selector reset applies border-box globally and is the standard in every modern CSS framework:
border-box makes sizing intuitive — a 50% wide element is always 50% of its parent
Margin is never included in the box dimensions regardless of box-sizing
The browser calculates layout using the computed width — border-box just changes how that value is interpreted
Margin Collapsing

Adjacent vertical margins of block elements collapse into a single margin equal to the larger of the two values. This is a deliberate CSS feature for body text spacing but surprises developers who expect margins to add.
When does margin collapse not happen?
Between flex or grid items — flex/grid formatting context never collapses margins
When an element has padding or border between the margins (separates parent/child margins)
Between an element and its first/last child when the parent has overflow: hidden or overflow: auto
Horizontal margins never collapse
Margins involving floated or absolutely-positioned elements never collapse
The display Property and Box Model Behavior
Not all elements behave the same in the box model. The display property controls whether and how an element's box participates in layout:
display value
width/height
vertical margin
Takes full width
block
Applied
Applied
Yes (fills container)
inline
Ignored
Ignored
No (wraps content)
inline-block
Applied
Applied
No (wraps content)
flex
Applied (to flex container)
Applied
Yes
grid
Applied (to grid container)
Applied
Yes
none
Element removed from layout
No
outline vs border
outline looks like border but is drawn outside the border area. It does not affect layout — adding or removing an outline never causes reflow. This makes it the default choice for focus rings:
outline does not support border-radius in older browsers (modern browsers do follow the element shape)
Removing outline: 0 without a replacement breaks keyboard navigation — always provide a visible focus indicator
outline-offset can be negative — a negative value moves the outline inside the border
Viewing the Box Model in DevTools

The browser DevTools Layout/Box Model panel shows the four areas as nested rectangles with their computed values. Select any element in the Inspector and look for the box model diagram in the right panel.
Hover over each area to highlight it on the page
Edit values directly in the diagram to test layout changes live
The computed width shown in the diagram respects box-sizing — it shows the element's actual rendered size
Angular Components and the Box Model

Angular components are rendered as custom HTML elements (e.g., <app-card>). These custom elements are inline by default — they do not participate in the block-level box model unless you set their display explicitly.
Angular's global styles.css is the right place to apply the universal box-sizing reset, which then applies inside all components:

With ViewEncapsulation.Emulated (default), the :host rule in a component's CSS file compiles to a scoped version targeting that component's element. The display: block pattern is the canonical way to ensure Angular components behave as block-level layout participants.
Common Box Model Pitfalls
Forgetting box-sizing: border-box — elements grow wider than expected when padding is added
Vertical margin collapse surprises — expected 32px gap between elements but only 16px because margins collapsed
Padding on inline elements — padding applies visually but does not affect the line box height or push adjacent content
width: 100% + padding overflow — without border-box, a 100%-wide element with padding exceeds the parent's width
margin: auto on inline elements — only works on block elements with explicit width
Angular :host element not block — component children may not lay out as expected if :host has no display value
CSS Logical Properties

Traditional box model properties (margin-top, padding-left) are tied to physical directions. CSS logical properties map to the start/end of the writing direction, making them correct for both left-to-right (LTR) and right-to-left (RTL) layouts:
Physical property
Logical equivalent
Maps to in LTR
margin-top
margin-block-start
top
margin-bottom
margin-block-end
bottom
margin-left
margin-inline-start
left
margin-right
margin-inline-end
right
padding-left
padding-inline-start
left
padding-right
padding-inline-end
right
width
inline-size
width
height
block-size
height
Logical properties are supported in all modern browsers
Angular Material uses logical properties internally for bidirectional (BiDi) support
Use logical properties for any component that may be used in RTL apps
Physical properties are still fine for one-off decorative spacing that does not change with direction
Auto Margins in Flex and Grid
margin: auto in a flex or grid container absorbs all available free space in the given direction. This is the simplest way to push an element to the opposite side:
margin: auto in flex/grid does not cause margin collapse
align-self: auto (flex) and margin: auto on the cross-axis are equivalent for single children
An element with margin: auto in a flex container ignores justify-content and align-items for that element
Sizing with min/max Constraints
The box model is not limited to fixed widths. min-width, max-width, min-height, and max-height add constraints that make layouts responsive without media queries:
The clamp() function combines min, ideal, and max in one expression — useful for fluid sizing:
Building a Card Component — Full Box Model Example
This complete example shows how box model properties interact in a typical Angular card component:

Box Model in Practice

Understanding the box model makes sizing and spacing predictable.
Key techniques used in this example:
display: block on :host ensures the component element participates in block layout
Negative margins on .card__image bleed past the card's padding to the edges
margin: 0 0 8px 0 resets the heading's default top margin and adds controlled bottom spacing
Negative margins on .card__footer bleed the footer border to the card edges
calc(100% + 48px) computes the bleed width relative to the card's content width
Overflow — When Content Escapes the Box
When content is larger than its container, the overflow property controls what happens:
overflow value
Behavior
visible (default)
Content renders outside the box — does not clip
hidden
Clips content at the box edge; also creates a block formatting context (prevents margin collapse)
scroll
Always shows scrollbars (even when unnecessary on some platforms)
auto
Shows scrollbars only when content overflows (preferred)
clip
Clips content like hidden but does not create a BFC — no scrolling possible
overflow: hidden on a container prevents child elements from visually escaping
overflow: hidden also prevents margin collapse — useful for ensuring a container 'contains' its children's margins
text-overflow: ellipsis requires overflow: hidden and white-space: nowrap to work
overflow-x and overflow-y can be set independently
Summary

Every HTML element occupies a rectangular box made of four areas: content (text/images), padding (space inside the border), border (the visible frame), and margin (space outside the element). The box-sizing property controls whether width/height measure just the content (content-box default) or the entire element including padding and border (border-box). Margins on adjacent block elements collapse vertically. outline is drawn outside the box and does not affect layout, making it the correct property for focus rings. In Angular, set display: block and box-sizing: border-box on :host to ensure component elements participate in layout predictably.
